questions.question: Why was the traveler hungry?
options: He had no food and had been traveling for days
He was saving his food for later

questions.question: What did the woman do when the traveler asked for food?
options: She invited him in for dinner
She said she had no food and shut the door
She gave him a big meal

questions.question: What did the traveler notice about the village?
options: It was empty with no people
He could smell food and see gardens
All the houses were broken down

questions.question: Why wouldn't the villagers share their food?
options: They were afraid more hungry people would come

questions.question: What did the traveler say his stone could do?
How did the villagers feel about the stone soup?
options: They thought it was very interesting

questions.question: What was the first ingredient the traveler asked for?
How did the villagers respond to the traveler's requests?
options: They told him to go away

questions.question: What brought more villagers to the town square?
options: The delicious smell of the soup

questions.question: How were the villagers feeling as they waited?
What did the traveler do with his stone when the soup was ready?
options: He left it in the soup
He put it safely in his pocket

questions.question: What was the real magic in the story?

informationalTexts.title: Rain
informationalTexts.content:
1. a) the sun heats up water and turns it into water vapor
2. a) it gives plants water and fills up rivers and lakes

informationalTexts.title: Police Officers
informationalTexts.content:
1. a) to keep communities safe
2. a) neighborhoods in cars, on foot, or on bicycles

informationalTexts.title: Firefighters
informationalTexts.content:
Firefighters are brave people who risk their lives to put out fires and help people during emergencies. When there is a fire, firefighters rush to the scene in fire trucks equipped with hoses, ladders, and other special tools. They wear heavy protective gear to keep themselves safe from heat, smoke, and dangerous chemicals. Firefighters work as a team to rescue people and save buildings from being destroyed by fire.
Besides fighting fires, firefighters respond to many other types of emergencies. They help people who are trapped in car accidents, rescue cats stuck in trees, and assist during natural disasters like floods or earthquakes. Many firefighters also teach fire safety to children and adults, showing them how to prevent fires and what to do if a fire starts. Becoming a firefighter requires special training and physical fitness because the job can be very demanding.

questions.question: What protective gear do firefighters wear?
options: heavy protective gear to keep safe from heat and smoke

questions.question: What other emergencies do firefighters help with?
options: car accidents, rescues, and natural disasters

informationalTexts.title: Snow
informationalTexts.content:
Snow forms when water vapor in clouds freezes into tiny ice crystals instead of turning into raindrops. This happens when the temperature in the clouds is below freezing. The ice crystals stick together as they fall through the cold air, creating snowflakes. Each snowflake has a unique shape with six sides, and no two snowflakes are exactly alike!
Snow is important for many reasons. It provides water for plants and animals when it melts in the spring. Many places depend on melted snow for their drinking water and to water crops. Snow also helps keep the ground warm in winter by acting like a blanket over plants and small animals. Some animals, like polar bears and penguins, need snow and ice to survive in their natural habitats.

questions.question: How does snow form?
options: when water vapor freezes into ice crystals in cold clouds

questions.question: Why is snow important?
options: it's only good for making snowmen
it provides water when it melts and helps keep the ground warm

informationalTexts.title: Giraffes
informationalTexts.content:
Giraffes are the tallest animals in the world, with long necks that can be up to 6 feet long! They live in Africa and use their height to eat leaves from tall acacia trees that other animals cannot reach. Giraffes have long, dark tongues that can be 18 inches long, which helps them grab leaves and branches. Their tall height also helps them see predators like lions from far away.
Even though giraffes are very tall, they are gentle animals that live in groups called herds. Baby giraffes are called calves, and they are already 6 feet tall when they are born! Giraffes have unique spotted patterns on their fur, and each giraffe's spots are different, just like human fingerprints. They can run up to 35 miles per hour when they need to escape from danger.

questions.question: Why are giraffes so tall?
options: to eat leaves from tall trees that other animals can't reach

questions.question: What are baby giraffes called?

informationalTexts.title: Construction Workers
informationalTexts.content:
Construction workers are people who build houses, schools, roads, bridges, and other important structures that we use every day. They use many different tools and machines, from simple hammers and saws to huge cranes and bulldozers. Construction workers must be strong and skilled because their job involves heavy lifting, precise measurements, and working with dangerous equipment. They also need to work well as a team since most construction projects require many people working together.
There are many different types of construction workers with special skills. Carpenters work with wood to build frames for houses and make furniture. Electricians install wiring so buildings have electricity for lights and appliances. Plumbers put in pipes to bring clean water into buildings and take dirty water away. All construction workers must follow safety rules and wear protective gear like hard hats and safety boots to prevent injuries on job sites.

questions.question: What do construction workers build?
options: houses, schools, roads, bridges, and other structures

questions.question: What safety equipment do construction workers wear?

grammarLessons.content:
**Nouns**

This lesson focuses on Nouns. As you read today's story, pay attention to examples of this concept.

**Key Points:**
• Understand the concept
• Find examples in the story
• Practice using it correctly

**Practice:**
Apply what you learned by completing the exercises.

**Remember:** Nouns is an important language skill!

languageLessons.content:
Rhyming words sound the SAME at the end. They make poems and stories fun to read aloud!

**Rhyming Words:**
• cat, hat, mat, sat (all end with -at)
• play, day, say, way (all end with -ay)
• book, look, cook, took (all end with -ook)

**From today's story:**
Listen for words that rhyme as you read!

**Practice - Find the rhyme:**
1. Which word rhymes with "dog"? (log, big, cat)
2. Which word rhymes with "jump"? (run, bump, sit)
3. Which word rhymes with "bed"? (chair, red, sleep)

**Remember:** Listen to the END of words to find rhymes!
languageLessons.objective: Students will identify and create rhyming words.
